summary: Richard II was born at Bordeaux on 6 January 1367, the younger son of Edward, the Black Prince, and Joan of Kent, and succeeded his grandfather Edward III as king on 21 June 1377. He married Anne of Bohemia in 1382 and, after her death in 1394, Isabella of Valois in 1396, but had no children by either. Deposed by his cousin Henry Bolingbroke in September 1399, he died in captivity at Pontefract Castle on 14 February 1400.

citations.title: Dictionary of National Biography, 1885-1900/Richard II
citations.quote: He was born in the abbey of St. Andrew at Bordeaux on 6 Jan. 1367, and was baptised in the cathedral three days later by the archbishop.

citations.title: Richard II of England - Wikipedia
citations.quote: Reign: 21 June 1377 – 29 September 1399

uncertain: Chroniclers disagree on the manner of Richard's death at Pontefract: some accounts hold he refused food and starved himself in despair, others that he was deliberately starved by his captors, and a later tradition alleges violent murder. The exact circumstances are unresolved.

summary: Henry IV, known as Henry Bolingbroke, was born on 3 April 1367 at Bolingbroke Castle in Lincolnshire, the son of John of Gaunt and Blanche of Lancaster. He married Mary de Bohun in 1381, with whom he had six children before her death in 1394, and after forcing the deposition of his cousin Richard II he was crowned king on 13 October 1399. In 1403 he took as his second wife Joan of Navarre, widow of the Duke of Brittany. He died at Westminster on 20 March 1413.

summary: Henry V, sometimes called Henry of Monmouth, was born at Monmouth Castle, the eldest son of Henry IV and Mary de Bohun; sources disagree on whether the year was 1386 or 1387. He succeeded his father on 20 March 1413 and was crowned at Westminster on 9 April 1413. Under the Treaty of Troyes he married Catherine of Valois, daughter of Charles VI of France, at Troyes on 2 June 1420; their son, the future Henry VI, was born at Windsor on 6 December 1421. Henry V died at the Bois de Vincennes, near Paris, on 31 August 1422.

citations.title: Henry V of England - Wikipedia
citations.quote: her infant son, born at Windsor on 6 Dec

uncertain: Henry V's year of birth is disputed: the Dictionary of National Biography gives 9 August 1387 as the most commonly accepted date, while modern scholarship (reflected on Wikipedia) favours 16 September 1386. The dataset follows the modern consensus but the dispute is unresolved in the sources.

summary: Anne of Bohemia was born in Prague on 11 May 1366, daughter of the Holy Roman Emperor Charles IV and Elizabeth of Pomerania. She married Richard II of England on 20 January 1382 at Westminster Abbey, becoming his queen consort, and died childless of plague at Sheen Palace on 7 June 1394, aged 28.

summary: Isabella of Valois, daughter of Charles VI of France and Isabeau of Bavaria, was born on 9 November 1389. She married Richard II of England at Calais on 31 October 1396 as a child of six, becoming queen consort of England, though the marriage was never consummated because of her age and Richard's death in 1400. She later married Charles of Orleans and died in childbirth at Blois on 13 September 1409.

summary: Mary de Bohun, born about 1369/70, was a daughter of Humphrey de Bohun, 7th Earl of Hereford, and Joan FitzAlan. She married Henry Bolingbroke, later Henry IV, on 5 February 1381, and bore him six children including the future Henry V. She died at Peterborough Castle on 4 June 1394 while giving birth to her last child, Philippa, before her husband became king.

summary: Joan of Navarre was born about 1368 in Pamplona, daughter of Charles II of Navarre and Joan of Valois. She first married John IV, Duke of Brittany, in 1386, and after his death married Henry IV of England on 7 February 1403 at Winchester Cathedral, becoming queen consort though she had no children by Henry. She died at Havering-atte-Bower, Essex, on 10 June 1437.

summary: Thomas of Lancaster, Duke of Clarence, second son of Henry IV and Mary de Bohun, was born before Christmas 1387. He married Margaret Holland, widow of his uncle John Beaufort, Earl of Somerset, in late 1411, and was created Duke of Clarence, a title previously held by Lionel of Antwerp. He was killed at the Battle of Baugé in Anjou on 22 March 1421, unhorsed and finished off by Scottish knights fighting in French service.

summary: John of Lancaster, Duke of Bedford, third son of Henry IV and Mary de Bohun, was born on 20 June 1389. Created Duke of Bedford by his brother Henry V in 1414, he became regent of France for his nephew Henry VI after Henry V's death in 1422. He married Anne of Burgundy in 1423 and, after her death in 1432, Jacquetta of Luxembourg in 1433. He died at Rouen on 14 September 1435.

summary: Humphrey, Duke of Gloucester, youngest son of Henry IV and Mary de Bohun, was born on 3 October 1390. Created Duke of Gloucester in 1414, he married Jacqueline, Countess of Hainaut, in 1423, a marriage annulled in 1428 after papal ruling upheld her earlier marriage; he then married Eleanor Cobham in 1428, a marriage annulled in 1441. He died at Bury St Edmunds, Suffolk, on 23 February 1447.

summary: Blanche of England, elder daughter of Henry IV and Mary de Bohun, was born in the spring of 1392. She married Louis III, Elector Palatine, at Cologne Cathedral on 6 July 1402. She died at Haguenau on 22 May 1409, while expecting her third child.

summary: Philippa of England, younger daughter of Henry IV and Mary de Bohun, was born on 4 June 1394, the day her mother died giving birth to her. She married Erik of Pomerania, king of Denmark, Norway and Sweden, at Lund Cathedral on 26 October 1406. She died at Vadstena Abbey in Sweden on 5 January 1430.

summary: Katherine Swynford, born about 1350, daughter of Sir Payne Roelt, a herald from Hainault, first married Sir Hugh Swynford, who died in 1372. She subsequently became the long-standing mistress of John of Gaunt, Duke of Lancaster, bearing him four children before he married her at Lincoln Cathedral on 13 January 1396. Their children, the Beauforts, were legitimated by papal bull later that year and by royal proclamation in 1397. She died at Lincoln on 10 May 1403.

uncertain: Sources vary slightly on Katherine's birth year (c. 1349 vs. c. 1350) and on the exact date of her first marriage to Hugh Swynford (the DNB says 'in or shortly before 1367'; other accounts place it as early as 1362/63).

summary: John Beaufort, eldest child of John of Gaunt and Katherine Swynford, was born about 1373. Declared legitimate by King Richard II in 1397, he was created Earl of Somerset on 10 February that year and shortly afterwards married Margaret Holland, niece of Richard II. He died on 16 March 1410 at the Hospital of St Katharine's by the Tower, London.

citations.title: John Beaufort, 1st Earl of Somerset - Wikipedia
citations.quote: The Beaufort children were declared legitimate twice by parliament, first during the reign of King Richard II, in 1397

uncertain: In 1407 Henry IV had the 1397 legitimation of his Beaufort half-siblings reaffirmed by Parliament but with a proviso barring them from claiming the crown; Wikipedia's House of Beaufort article states 'in 1406, Henry IV decided that although the Beauforts were legitimate, their line could not be used to make any claim to the throne', but the legal force and later relevance of this bar (invoked against the Beauforts' Tudor descendants) is debated by historians.

summary: Henry Beaufort, second son of John of Gaunt and Katherine Swynford, was born about 1375. He was appointed Bishop of Winchester on 19 November 1404 and served three times as Lord Chancellor of England, and was elevated to cardinal by Pope Martin V in 1426. He died at Wolvesey Castle, Winchester, on 11 April 1447.

summary: Thomas Beaufort, youngest son of John of Gaunt and Katherine Swynford, was born about January 1377. A career soldier, he served as captain of Harfleur after its capture in 1415 and led sieges at Evreux, Ivry and Rouen, and was created Duke of Exeter on 18 November 1416. He married Margaret Neville, and died at Greenwich manor, Kent, on 30 or 31 December 1426.

summary: Catherine of Valois was born on 27 October 1401, daughter of Charles VI of France and Isabeau of Bavaria. Under the Treaty of Troyes she married Henry V of England at Troyes on 2 June 1420, and on 6 December 1421 bore him a son, the future Henry VI. After Henry's death in 1422 she entered into a relationship, and reportedly a secret marriage of unknown date, with the Welshman Owen Tudor, by whom she had several more children. She died in London on 3 January 1437, shortly after childbirth.

summary: Henry VI was born at Windsor Castle on 6 December 1421, the only child of Henry V and Catherine of Valois. He succeeded to the English throne at the age of eight months on 1 September 1422, the day after his father's death, becoming the youngest person ever to succeed to the English throne. This dataset ends with Henry V's death in 1422; Henry VI's subsequent reign and life fall outside its scope.

uncertain: This record covers only Henry VI's birth and accession, the point at which this dataset (Richard II–Henry V, ending 1422) closes. His subsequent reign, deposition (1461), brief readeption (1470–71), and death are outside this shard's scope and are left for a later shard covering the Yorkist period.

summary: Philippa, 5th Countess of Ulster, was born at Eltham Palace on 16 August 1355, the only child of Lionel of Antwerp, Duke of Clarence, and Elizabeth de Burgh, and so the eldest grandchild of Edward III. She married Edmund Mortimer, 3rd Earl of March, in 1369, and their son Roger Mortimer inherited the earldom. She died young, by December 1379 at the latest. Because her father was Edward III's second surviving son, her descendants' claim through her was later cited as senior to that of the Lancastrian kings descended from Edward III's third son, John of Gaunt.

citations.title: Philippa, 5th Countess of Ulster - Wikipedia
citations.quote: Her father was the second surviving son of King Edward III of England.

citations.quote: the Yorkist claim to the crown was based on descent from Edward III through Philippa...her son Roger Mortimer...and granddaughter Anne Mortimer

uncertain: Her exact death date is unclear: Wikipedia gives c. 1377 (aged 22) but notes she 'certainly died by December 1379'.

name: Roger Mortimer, 4th Earl of March

citations.title: Roger Mortimer, 4th Earl of March - Wikipedia

summary: Roger Mortimer, 4th Earl of March, was born on 11 April 1374 at Usk in Monmouthshire, son of Edmund Mortimer, 3rd Earl of March, and Philippa Plantagenet, herself granddaughter of Edward III through his second surviving son, Lionel of Antwerp. He married Alianore (Eleanor) Holland in 1388. Some sources hold that Richard II proclaimed him heir presumptive to the throne in 1385, though this is disputed by modern historians. He was killed fighting at the Battle of Kellistown in County Carlow, Ireland, on 20 July 1398.

citations.title: Roger Mortimer, 4th Earl of March - Wikipedia
citations.quote: Edmund Mortimer, 3rd Earl of March

citations.title: Roger Mortimer, 4th Earl of March - Wikipedia
citations.quote: in October 1385 Mortimer was proclaimed by the king as heir presumptive to the crown

uncertain: Whether Richard II ever formally proclaimed Roger Mortimer heir presumptive is disputed: G. E. Cokayne states 'in October 1385 Mortimer was proclaimed by the king as heir presumptive to the crown', but the historian R. R. Davies is quoted as calling 'the story that Richard publicly proclaimed Mortimer as heir presumptive in Parliament in October 1385...baseless'. Regardless, as descendants of Edward III's second surviving son Lionel of Antwerp, the Mortimers had a genealogically senior claim to that of the Lancastrian kings (descended from Edward III's third son, John of Gaunt) — a claim later inherited by the Yorkist line.

name: Edmund Mortimer, 5th Earl of March

citations.title: Edmund Mortimer, 5th Earl of March - Wikipedia
citations.quote: 6 November 1391

citations.title: Edmund Mortimer, 5th Earl of March - Wikipedia
citations.quote: On 18 or 19 January 1425 died of plague at Trim Castle

citations.title: Edmund Mortimer, 5th Earl of March - Wikipedia
citations.quote: On 17 January 1415 Edmund obtained a papal dispensation...to marry his second cousin once removed, Anne Stafford

summary: Edmund Mortimer, 5th Earl of March, was born on 6 November 1391 at New Forest, County Westmeath, Ireland, son of Roger Mortimer, 4th Earl of March, and Eleanor Holland. As a great-great-grandson of Edward III, he was regarded as heir presumptive to Richard II at the time of Richard's deposition by Henry IV in 1399, and was kept, though treated honourably, in wardship during Henry IV's reign. He obtained a papal dispensation to marry Anne Stafford in January 1415, and remained loyal to Henry V, never asserting any claim to the throne even though the Southampton Plot of 1415 sought to place him on it. He died of plague at Trim Castle, Ireland, on 18 or 19 January 1425.

citations.quote: he was heir presumptive to King Richard II of England...when the latter was deposed in favour of Henry IV

summary: Anne Mortimer was born on 27 December 1388, daughter of Roger Mortimer, 4th Earl of March, and Eleanor Holland. She married Richard of Conisburgh, later Earl of Cambridge, around early 1408, a union validated by papal dispensation on 23 May 1408. She died on 22 September 1411, shortly after giving birth to her son Richard, later 3rd Duke of York, whose descent from her carried the Mortimer claim to the throne into the Yorkist cause of the Wars of the Roses.

citations.title: Anne Mortimer - Wikipedia
citations.quote: It was her line of descent which gave the Yorkist dynasty its claim to the throne.

uncertain: The precise date of Anne's marriage to Richard of Conisburgh is imprecisely recorded: sources place it 'around early 1408 (probably after 8 January)', with papal dispensation validating it only on 23 May 1408.

summary: Richard of Conisburgh, 3rd Earl of Cambridge, was born on 20 July 1385 at Conisbrough Castle, Yorkshire, younger son of Edmund of Langley, 1st Duke of York, and Isabella of Castile. He married Anne Mortimer around 1408; their son Richard, Duke of York, later pursued the Mortimer claim to the throne. In 1415 Richard conspired with Lord Scrope and Sir Thomas Grey to depose Henry V and place his late wife's brother, Edmund Mortimer, 5th Earl of March, on the throne. The plot was discovered, and he was beheaded at Southampton on 5 August 1415.

name: Edward of Norwich, 2nd Duke of York

citations.title: Edward of Norwich, 2nd Duke of York - Wikipedia
Edward of Norwich, 2nd Duke of York - Wikipedia
citations.quote: He commanded the right wing at the Battle of Agincourt on 25 October 1415, during which he became the highest-ranking English casualty.

summary: Edward of Norwich, 2nd Duke of York, was born about 1373 at Kings Langley, Hertfordshire, elder son of Edmund of Langley, 1st Duke of York, and Isabella of Castile. He succeeded his father as Duke of York on 1 August 1402. At the Battle of Agincourt on 25 October 1415 he commanded the right wing of the English army and was killed, becoming its highest-ranking English casualty; one account holds his intervention saved Henry V's life at the cost of his own. He was buried at the Church of St Mary and All Saints, Fotheringhay, on 1 December 1415.
